Peste 1.500 de antreprenori, investitori și profesioniști sunt așteptați la Romexpo pentru tentativa oficială de doborâre a recordului mondial Guinness World Records pentru cea mai mare întâlnire de speed networking organizată vreodată. Drept represalii, președintele american a anunțat că renunță și el la planul de a-și boteza câinele „Băluță”. „Aveam deja zgarda comandată, scria frumos «Băluță» pe ea, dar se pare că […] The post Supărat că parcul din Berceni nu-i va mai purta numele, Trump nu-şi mai botează câinele Băluţă appeared first on Times New Roman .

Primăria Capitalei anunţă că va instala vara aceasta un focar de hantavirus pe vaporaşul din Herăstrău, fără să crească preţul unei curse standard, de 25 de minute. „Evident, nu putem aduce pe vaporaşul […] The post Pentru cine nu îşi permite croaziere de lux, s-a băgat hantavirus pe vaporaşul din Herăstrău appeared first on Times New Roman .

Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

medical.title: Cracking the code of p53 fragility: Why the genome guardian is prone to failure
medical.summary: The protein p53 is often called the guardian of the genome for its central role in preventing cancer. Yet paradoxically, it is also one of the most frequently mutated and dysfunctional proteins in human tumors.

medical.title: Scientists discover why Alzheimer’s risk hits women so much harder
medical.summary: Women may be especially sensitive to the effects of common dementia risk factors, according to a new UC San Diego study of over 17,000 adults. Researchers say tailoring prevention strategies specifically for women could be key to reducing Alzheimer’s risk.

medical.title: Scientists found a hidden Alzheimer’s trigger and shut it down
medical.summary: A newly identified enzyme called IDOL could become a major new target in the fight against Alzheimer’s disease. Researchers found that removing it from neurons sharply reduced amyloid plaques and improved key brain processes linked to resilience and communication between cells. The discovery may lead to future treatments that go beyond slowing Alzheimer’s — potentially helping protect the brain from further decline.

medical.title: Harnessing brain imaging to shift the mental health paradigm
medical.summary: Stanford Medicine professor Leanne Williams talks about her work leveraging a data-driven approach to enhance the diagnosis and treatment of psychiatric conditions.

medical.title: Ozone pollution limits lung benefits of exercise in children, study finds
medical.summary: Vigorous physical activity promotes lung growth in children, but these benefits are reduced by ozone pollution, according to new research presented at the ATS 2026 International Conference. The findings suggest that the health benefits of exercising at levels recommended by the World Health Organization may be limited by environmental conditions.

medical.title: Brain's chemical brake hides a second power, and it could reshape how mental disorders are treated
medical.summary: An important chemical messenger that typically inhibits brain activity might sometimes do the opposite, according to new Yale School of Medicine (YSM) research. One way that brain cells communicate is through chemical messengers called neurotransmitters. Most research indicates that the neurotransmitter GABA (gamma-aminobutyric acid) quiets brain signals, serving as the system's brakes.

medical.title: In the digital health era, can we do better than a consent form?
medical.summary: Camille Nebeker, EdD, MS, a professor at the UC San Diego Herbert Wertheim School of Public Health and Human Longevity Science and an affiliate of both the Design Lab and Qualcomm Institute, with decades of experience conducting empirical research on digital health research ethics, argues that there needs to be a rethink about informed consent in the digital health era.

medical.title: 3,798 dengue cases reported in United States in 2024
medical.summary: In 2024, there were 3,798 dengue cases reported, 97.2% of which were associated with travel outside the reporting jurisdiction, according to research published in the Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report.

medical.title: Children of parents with severe mental illness face higher risk of cognitive difficulties, study finds
medical.summary: A new study led by Murdoch University has found that children of parents with severe mental illness are more likely to experience cognitive difficulties. The study, "Cognitive performance in offspring of parents with severe mental illness: a meta-analysis," is published in the journal Psychological Medicine.

medical.title: Autism social differences emerge early but can change considerably by adulthood, research suggests
medical.summary: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental condition characterized by differences in behavior, social interactions, communication, and sensory perceptions. Some autistic individuals find communicating and connecting with others more challenging than others, yet how these differences in social functioning emerge remains poorly understood.

medical.title: Researchers identify new drug targets for hard-to-treat cancers
medical.summary: Despite impressive innovations in medicine, most advanced-stage cancers still carry a grim prognosis. Developing more effective treatments requires a deeper understanding of the cellular processes that drive the formation and growth of common cancers.

medical.title: Outbreak of rare Ebola's global risk is low but worries mount about its spread in Congo
medical.summary: The new outbreak of a rare Ebola type in Congo and Uganda poses low global risk, the World Health Organization said Wednesday, but responders on the ground described being underprotected and undertrained for a rapidly spreading virus with no approved medicine or vaccine.

medical.title: Q&A: What is in the new American Heart Association dietary recommendations?
medical.summary: Heart disease is the leading cause of death in the United States, and altering your diet is the most effective way to reduce the risk of heart disease, according to the American Heart Association. To that end, the American Heart Association regularly issues dietary recommendations for maintaining a healthy heart. Kristina Petersen, associate professor of nutritional sciences at Penn State, was part of the team of researchers who drafted the new recommendations.

medical.title: Scientists use light to create tiny molecules that could transform medicine
medical.summary: Researchers have developed a light-driven method for creating tiny, high-energy “housane” molecules that are valuable for drug development and materials science. These compact ring-shaped structures are difficult to produce because of the intense internal strain they contain. By using photocatalysis and carefully tuning the starting molecules, the team managed to guide the reaction into a clean and efficient pathway.

medical.title: A new approach to fostering is taking shape in England
medical.summary: It takes a village to raise a child, or so the proverb goes. A new approach to fostering is embracing that idea The post A new approach to fostering is taking shape in England appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Scientists discover vitamin B2 may help cancer cells survive
medical.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising dark side to vitamin B2: it may help cancer cells stay alive. The vitamin supports a cellular shield that protects tumors from ferroptosis, a form of programmed cell death linked to cancer suppression. In lab tests, researchers used a vitamin B2-like compound called roseoflavin to break down that protection and trigger cancer cell death.

medical.title: The brain’s “feel good” chemical may be secretly fueling tinnitus
medical.summary: Scientists have uncovered evidence that serotonin — the same brain chemical boosted by many antidepressants — may actually worsen tinnitus. Using advanced light-based brain stimulation in mice, researchers identified a serotonin-driven circuit linked directly to tinnitus-like behavior. The findings may explain why some people experience louder ringing in their ears while taking SSRIs.

medical.title: Scientists successfully transfer longevity gene and extend lifespan
medical.summary: Scientists at the University of Rochester pulled off a remarkable experiment: they transferred a longevity-related gene from the famously long-lived naked mole rat into mice, and the mice ended up healthier and lived longer. The special gene boosts production of a substance called high molecular weight hyaluronic acid, which appears to protect against cancer, reduce inflammation, and support healthier aging. The modified mice showed stronger resistance to tumors, healthier guts, and lower levels of age-related inflammation.

science.title: An explanation for the massive black holes the JWST found in the early universe
science.summary: One of the most puzzling findings from the JWST's observations of the early universe is the size of black holes. According to our understanding of black hole growth, these early black holes are far more massive than expected. Astronomers expected the unexpected from JWST, and it has delivered. Now the challenge is to update models of the universe to include these new observations.

science.title: Portugal burial reveals first known bone dental bridge in national archaeological record
science.summary: The first documented case of a fixed bone bridge unearthed in Portugal was presented in the International Journal of Osteoarchaeology by researchers Ms. Steffi Vassallo and her colleagues. The item is estimated to date to the 19th century and is likely to have served a more aesthetic than functional purpose.

science.title: Image: NASA's Psyche mission captures Mars' Huygens Crater
science.summary: Captured by the multispectral imager instrument on NASA's Psyche mission, this is an enhanced-color view of the large double-ring crater Huygens (upper right; about 290 miles, or 470 kilometers, in diameter) and the surrounding heavily cratered southern highlands near 15 degrees south latitude. The various colors in this dramatic scene are likely due to differences in the compositional properties of dust, sand, and bedrock in this ancient terrain. The image scale is around 2,200 feet (670 meters) per pixel.

science.title: Lab fish cycles are hours out of sync with natural ones, researchers discover
science.summary: When researchers moved medaka—a fish commonly used in experiments—out of the lab and into more natural conditions, their reproductive clock shifted by hours, suggesting that laboratory findings may not fully capture their natural reproductive timing.

science.title: Scientists discover massive natural hydrogen source beneath Canada
science.summary: Scientists in Canada have discovered that ancient underground rocks are naturally producing hydrogen gas — and lots of it. Measurements from mine boreholes in Ontario show the gas can flow continuously for years, offering a potential new source of clean energy called “white hydrogen.” Researchers say this hidden resource could help power industries and remote communities while cutting carbon emissions and reducing dependence on fossil fuels.

science.title: Tiny sea creature Porpita porpita may live adrift at sea for years longer than previously thought
science.summary: A new study of the blue button (Porpita porpita), a small and elusive sea creature which lives on the surface of the ocean, has found that it may live for several years adrift at sea, much longer than previously estimated.

science.title: Image: NASA's Psyche mission images the crescent of Mars
science.summary: This view of a crescent Mars was captured on May 15, 2026, at about 5:03 a.m. PDT by NASA's Psyche mission as it approached the planet for a gravity assist. Captured by the spacecraft's multispectral imager instrument, this was the last view of the whole planet before it began to overfill the field of view of the camera.

science.title: Image: NASA's Psyche mission spies Mars' wind-blown craters during close approach
science.summary: This view of the Martian surface, captured by NASA's Psyche spacecraft on May 15, 2026, shows streaks that have formed due to wind blowing over impact craters in the Syrtis Major region. The image scale is nearly 1,200 feet (360 meters) per pixel. The wind streaks extend to about 30 miles (50 kilometers) long, and the large craters near the center-bottom of the scene average about 30 miles in diameter.

science.title: How face-building genes get ready early: Genome folding may prime crucial DNA switches
science.summary: Early in development, a group of migrating cells called cranial neural crest cells go on to form many different parts of the face, including the nose, jaw, ears, and throat. To build these structures correctly, genes must switch on in the right cells at the right time. But many of the DNA switches that control those genes sit far away on the genome, and scientists still know little about how genes find and communicate with these distant switches during development.

science.title: Naturally occurring soil fungi could boost rice yields while reducing reliance on synthetic fertilizers
science.summary: Field trials in India show that bio-fertilizers containing naturally occurring soil fungi enhance growth in rice plants. This may offer a pathway to reducing farmers' reliance on synthetic fertilizers, and reducing greenhouse gas emissions in rice farming. The trials, led by researchers at the University of Cambridge in partnership with U.K. rice brand Tilda and basmati farmers in India, tested fungi-based bio-fertilizers under real farming conditions.

science.title: Could future Mars settlers print their own tools?
science.summary: If humans one day settle Mars, they will need tools and parts to build structures on the planet. Carrying heavy, bulky supplies 34 million miles from Earth would be impractical. A better plan, says Zane Mebruer, a recent graduate of the U of A, would be 3D printing items on the Red Planet. His new research, completed while he was an honors undergraduate in mechanical engineering at the U of A, suggests it may be possible.

science.title: Argentine researchers collect rodents for hantavirus tests
science.summary: Argentine scientists on Tuesday began collecting rodents in the woods around Ushuaia to search for carriers of hantavirus in the area from which the virus-stricken MV Hondius set sail.

science.title: Zoo reaches historic milestone for Puerto Rican crested toad conservation efforts with more than 12,000 tadpoles
science.summary: Behind the scenes at Brookfield Zoo Chicago, a record-breaking conservation milestone is helping secure the future of one of the world's most imperiled amphibians. Months of meticulous care and coordination enabled Brookfield Zoo Chicago to successfully breed and raise 12,244 Puerto Rican crested toad tadpoles to be released in the wild, supporting species recovery efforts.

science.title: Sky bridges, citizen science protect endangered Malaysia monkeys
science.summary: A graceful black monkey edges across a swaying red rope bridge strung over a busy residential road in Malaysia's Penang, watched by local conservationists who carefully record her movements.

science.title: Scientists found a giant magnetic “twist” hidden inside the Milky Way
science.summary: Astronomers have uncovered a strange magnetic “flip” hidden inside the Milky Way. Using a new radio telescope, researchers mapped the galaxy’s magnetic field in unprecedented detail and discovered that a mysterious reversal in the Sagittarius Arm cuts diagonally across space. The finding could reshape how scientists understand the structure and future evolution of our galaxy.

science.title: Britain’s 11,000-year-old “oldest northerner” was a 3-year-old girl, DNA reveals
science.summary: Scientists have identified the oldest known human remains in Northern Britain as a young girl who lived around 11,000 years ago. Found in a Cumbrian cave and nicknamed the “Ossick Lass,” she was likely between 2.5 and 3.5 years old when she died. Nearby jewelry and evidence of multiple burials suggest the cave held deep spiritual importance for some of Britain’s earliest hunter-gatherers. The discovery is shedding new light on life — and death — just after the Ice Age.

science.title: Scientists discover giant “last titan” dinosaur, Southeast Asia’s largest ever
science.summary: A massive new dinosaur discovered in Thailand is rewriting Southeast Asia’s prehistoric history. The newly named Nagatitan chaiyaphumensis was a colossal long-necked sauropod that weighed around 27 tonnes and lived more than 100 million years ago. Scientists believe it may be the last giant sauropod ever to roam the region before rising seas transformed the landscape.

science.title: NASA’s Roman Space Telescope could reveal millions of invisible neutron stars
science.summary: NASA’s Roman Space Telescope could expose a vast hidden population of neutron stars lurking unseen across the Milky Way. By detecting subtle shifts in starlight caused by gravity, the mission may identify and even weigh isolated neutron stars that are otherwise impossible to see. Scientists hope the discoveries will reveal how these extreme objects are born and why they are blasted through space at incredible speeds.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: E oficial: Guiness World Records vine în România să certifice cea mai mare întâlnire de Speed Networking din lume
mix_items.summary: Pe 27 mai 2026, cu ajutorul Romanian Business Club, Bucureștiul va deveni epicentrul antreprenoriatului mondial. Peste 1.500 de antreprenori, investitori și profesioniști sunt așteptați la Romexpo pentru tentativa oficială de doborâre a recordului mondial Guinness World Records pentru cea mai mare întâlnire de speed networking organizată vreodată. Mecanismul Guinness World Records: DISCIPLINĂ PENTRU SUCCES Pentru …

mix_items.title: Animest SUPER prinde avânt: Maraton de evenimente dedicate cinematografiei incluzive în luna mai
mix_items.summary: Programul național Animest SUPER, dezvoltat de Asociația Animest, își continuă parcursul în luna mai cu o agendă densă, plină de întâlniri și proiecții speciale create cu un singur scop: eliminarea… Articolul Animest SUPER prinde avânt: Maraton de evenimente dedicate cinematografiei incluzive în luna mai apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Gen Z redescoperă filmele cult în cinema: de ce tinerii aleg producții lansate înainte să se nască
mix_items.summary: București, mai 2026 — Filme precum Blade Runner, Fight Club, The Matrix, Top Gun sau Diavolul se îmbracă de la Prada continuă să fascineze și să atragă un public numeros… Articolul Gen Z redescoperă filmele cult în cinema: de ce tinerii aleg producții lansate înainte să se nască apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Supărat că parcul din Berceni nu-i va mai purta numele, Trump nu-şi mai botează câinele Băluţă
mix_items.summary: Donald Trump s-a declarat profund dezamăgit după ce a aflat că primarul Daniel Băluță nu va mai redenumi un parc din Berceni cu numele său. Drept represalii, președintele american a anunțat că renunță și el la planul de a-și boteza câinele „Băluță”. „Aveam deja zgarda comandată, scria frumos «Băluță» pe ea, dar se pare că […] The post Supărat că parcul din Berceni nu-i va mai purta numele, Trump nu-şi mai botează câinele Băluţă appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: În România, mulți copii încă se tem să vorbească despre abuzurile prin care trec. Conform unui studiu al Fundației World... Articolul Doar 1 din 2 copii ar anunța poliția în cazul unui abuz. World Vision România: Copiii care raportează trebuie sprijiniți și încurajați, nu umiliți public. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: După ce pe TikTok au început să circule mii de filmulețe în care se spune că francezii, după apa din lacul Vidraru, ne fură și apa din lacul Herăstrău, Primăria Capitalei a ținut să ofere o dezmințire fermă: „Apa din Herăstrău nu pleacă spre Franța, ca să fie clar! Ea pleacă spre Germania. Haideți să […] The post PMB neagă că apa din lacul Herăstrău va ajunge în Franța: „Va ajunge în Germania!” appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor
mix_items.summary: Alexandra, adolescentă sprijinită de Fundația Inocenți, și-a recăpătat încrederea în sine și motivația pentru învățare prin suport educațional și emoțional. The post Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.summary: Locație: București (Satul SOS) Nivel carieră: Mid-level (3-5 ani experiență) Departament: Financiar / Contabilitate Tip job: Full-time Cine suntem noi? La SOS Satele Copiilor România, oferim o familie copiilor ajunși... The post Contabil appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... Articolul World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Băneasa Shopping City continuă să aducă mai aproape de public universul Formula 1
mix_items.summary: Băneasa Shopping City devine destinația în care publicul este invitat să descopere universul performanței din motorsport. În perioada 22-24 mai, vizitatorii pot admira monopostul de Formula 1 Ferrari SF-23, expus… Articolul Băneasa Shopping City continuă să aducă mai aproape de public universul Formula 1 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Tenorul ŞTEFAN von KORCH şi invitaţii au purtat publicul pe „Valurile Dunării” de Ziua Europei
mix_items.summary: Ziua Europei a prilejuit a doua ediție a concertului Valurile Dunării, in care tenorul gazdă Ștefan von Korch și soliștii invitații – Oana Maria Șerban și Fang Shuang ce a… Articolul Tenorul ŞTEFAN von KORCH şi invitaţii au purtat publicul pe „Valurile Dunării” de Ziua Europei apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: PENNY este retailerul cu cea mai mare cantitate de alimente donate către Băncile Regionale pentru Alimente în 2025 – 5.401 tone
mix_items.summary: Prin donații periodice din magazine și din toate cele cinci centre logistice din țară, PENNY a contribuit în 2025 cu 5.401 tone de alimente în valoare de peste 11 milioane… Articolul PENNY este retailerul cu cea mai mare cantitate de alimente donate către Băncile Regionale pentru Alimente în 2025 – 5.401 tone apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Pentru cine nu îşi permite croaziere de lux, s-a băgat hantavirus pe vaporaşul din Herăstrău
mix_items.summary: Veşti bune pentru bucureştenii care nu-şi permit croaziere fiţoase la Ushuaia, dar visează la răsfăţul de la bordul unui vas de lux! Primăria Capitalei anunţă că va instala vara aceasta un focar de hantavirus pe vaporaşul din Herăstrău, fără să crească preţul unei curse standard, de 25 de minute. „Evident, nu putem aduce pe vaporaşul […] The post Pentru cine nu îşi permite croaziere de lux, s-a băgat hantavirus pe vaporaşul din Herăstrău appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi
mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Psihopedagog/Pedagog
mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău Fii adultul care poate schimba viitorul unui copil Dacă ai răbdare, empatie și îți dorești un job cu adevărat valoros, în care fiecare zi contează pentru dezvoltarea unor... The post Psihopedagog/Pedagog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Cracking the code of p53 fragility: Why the genome guardian is prone to failure
mix_items.summary: The protein p53 is often called the guardian of the genome for its central role in preventing cancer. Yet paradoxically, it is also one of the most frequently mutated and dysfunctional proteins in human tumors.

mix_items.title: Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional
mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Agent telemarketing (team leader)
mix_items.summary: Locație: București Contract: Part time (remote) Credem în cauza SOS Satele Copiilor și știm că fiecare copil merită să crească într-o familie iubitoare. De 30 de ani SOS Satele Copiilor... The post Agent telemarketing (team leader) appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: An explanation for the massive black holes the JWST found in the early universe
mix_items.summary: One of the most puzzling findings from the JWST's observations of the early universe is the size of black holes. According to our understanding of black hole growth, these early black holes are far more massive than expected. Astronomers expected the unexpected from JWST, and it has delivered. Now the challenge is to update models of the universe to include these new observations.

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Portugal burial reveals first known bone dental bridge in national archaeological record
mix_items.summary: The first documented case of a fixed bone bridge unearthed in Portugal was presented in the International Journal of Osteoarchaeology by researchers Ms. Steffi Vassallo and her colleagues. The item is estimated to date to the 19th century and is likely to have served a more aesthetic than functional purpose.

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why Alzheimer’s risk hits women so much harder
mix_items.summary: Women may be especially sensitive to the effects of common dementia risk factors, according to a new UC San Diego study of over 17,000 adults. Researchers say tailoring prevention strategies specifically for women could be key to reducing Alzheimer’s risk.

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Image: NASA's Psyche mission captures Mars' Huygens Crater
mix_items.summary: Captured by the multispectral imager instrument on NASA's Psyche mission, this is an enhanced-color view of the large double-ring crater Huygens (upper right; about 290 miles, or 470 kilometers, in diameter) and the surrounding heavily cratered southern highlands near 15 degrees south latitude. The various colors in this dramatic scene are likely due to differences in the compositional properties of dust, sand, and bedrock in this ancient terrain. The image scale is around 2,200 feet (670 meters) per pixel.

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Lab fish cycles are hours out of sync with natural ones, researchers discover
mix_items.summary: When researchers moved medaka—a fish commonly used in experiments—out of the lab and into more natural conditions, their reproductive clock shifted by hours, suggesting that laboratory findings may not fully capture their natural reproductive timing.

mix_items.title: Scientists found a hidden Alzheimer’s trigger and shut it down
mix_items.summary: A newly identified enzyme called IDOL could become a major new target in the fight against Alzheimer’s disease. Researchers found that removing it from neurons sharply reduced amyloid plaques and improved key brain processes linked to resilience and communication between cells. The discovery may lead to future treatments that go beyond slowing Alzheimer’s — potentially helping protect the brain from further decline.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover massive natural hydrogen source beneath Canada
mix_items.summary: Scientists in Canada have discovered that ancient underground rocks are naturally producing hydrogen gas — and lots of it. Measurements from mine boreholes in Ontario show the gas can flow continuously for years, offering a potential new source of clean energy called “white hydrogen.” Researchers say this hidden resource could help power industries and remote communities while cutting carbon emissions and reducing dependence on fossil fuels.

mix_items.title: Harnessing brain imaging to shift the mental health paradigm
mix_items.summary: Stanford Medicine professor Leanne Williams talks about her work leveraging a data-driven approach to enhance the diagnosis and treatment of psychiatric conditions.

mix_items.title: Tiny sea creature Porpita porpita may live adrift at sea for years longer than previously thought
mix_items.summary: A new study of the blue button (Porpita porpita), a small and elusive sea creature which lives on the surface of the ocean, has found that it may live for several years adrift at sea, much longer than previously estimated.

mix_items.title: Ozone pollution limits lung benefits of exercise in children, study finds
mix_items.summary: Vigorous physical activity promotes lung growth in children, but these benefits are reduced by ozone pollution, according to new research presented at the ATS 2026 International Conference. The findings suggest that the health benefits of exercising at levels recommended by the World Health Organization may be limited by environmental conditions.

mix_items.title: Image: NASA's Psyche mission images the crescent of Mars
mix_items.summary: This view of a crescent Mars was captured on May 15, 2026, at about 5:03 a.m. PDT by NASA's Psyche mission as it approached the planet for a gravity assist. Captured by the spacecraft's multispectral imager instrument, this was the last view of the whole planet before it began to overfill the field of view of the camera.

mix_items.title: Brain's chemical brake hides a second power, and it could reshape how mental disorders are treated
mix_items.summary: An important chemical messenger that typically inhibits brain activity might sometimes do the opposite, according to new Yale School of Medicine (YSM) research. One way that brain cells communicate is through chemical messengers called neurotransmitters. Most research indicates that the neurotransmitter GABA (gamma-aminobutyric acid) quiets brain signals, serving as the system's brakes.

mix_items.title: Image: NASA's Psyche mission spies Mars' wind-blown craters during close approach
mix_items.summary: This view of the Martian surface, captured by NASA's Psyche spacecraft on May 15, 2026, shows streaks that have formed due to wind blowing over impact craters in the Syrtis Major region. The image scale is nearly 1,200 feet (360 meters) per pixel. The wind streaks extend to about 30 miles (50 kilometers) long, and the large craters near the center-bottom of the scene average about 30 miles in diameter.

mix_items.title: In the digital health era, can we do better than a consent form?
mix_items.summary: Camille Nebeker, EdD, MS, a professor at the UC San Diego Herbert Wertheim School of Public Health and Human Longevity Science and an affiliate of both the Design Lab and Qualcomm Institute, with decades of experience conducting empirical research on digital health research ethics, argues that there needs to be a rethink about informed consent in the digital health era.

mix_items.title: 3,798 dengue cases reported in United States in 2024
mix_items.summary: In 2024, there were 3,798 dengue cases reported, 97.2% of which were associated with travel outside the reporting jurisdiction, according to research published in the Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report.

mix_items.title: How face-building genes get ready early: Genome folding may prime crucial DNA switches
mix_items.summary: Early in development, a group of migrating cells called cranial neural crest cells go on to form many different parts of the face, including the nose, jaw, ears, and throat. To build these structures correctly, genes must switch on in the right cells at the right time. But many of the DNA switches that control those genes sit far away on the genome, and scientists still know little about how genes find and communicate with these distant switches during development.

mix_items.title: Children of parents with severe mental illness face higher risk of cognitive difficulties, study finds
mix_items.summary: A new study led by Murdoch University has found that children of parents with severe mental illness are more likely to experience cognitive difficulties. The study, "Cognitive performance in offspring of parents with severe mental illness: a meta-analysis," is published in the journal Psychological Medicine.

mix_items.title: Naturally occurring soil fungi could boost rice yields while reducing reliance on synthetic fertilizers
mix_items.summary: Field trials in India show that bio-fertilizers containing naturally occurring soil fungi enhance growth in rice plants. This may offer a pathway to reducing farmers' reliance on synthetic fertilizers, and reducing greenhouse gas emissions in rice farming. The trials, led by researchers at the University of Cambridge in partnership with U.K. rice brand Tilda and basmati farmers in India, tested fungi-based bio-fertilizers under real farming conditions.

mix_items.title: Autism social differences emerge early but can change considerably by adulthood, research suggests
mix_items.summary: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental condition characterized by differences in behavior, social interactions, communication, and sensory perceptions. Some autistic individuals find communicating and connecting with others more challenging than others, yet how these differences in social functioning emerge remains poorly understood.

mix_items.title: Researchers identify new drug targets for hard-to-treat cancers
mix_items.summary: Despite impressive innovations in medicine, most advanced-stage cancers still carry a grim prognosis. Developing more effective treatments requires a deeper understanding of the cellular processes that drive the formation and growth of common cancers.

mix_items.title: Could future Mars settlers print their own tools?
mix_items.summary: If humans one day settle Mars, they will need tools and parts to build structures on the planet. Carrying heavy, bulky supplies 34 million miles from Earth would be impractical. A better plan, says Zane Mebruer, a recent graduate of the U of A, would be 3D printing items on the Red Planet. His new research, completed while he was an honors undergraduate in mechanical engineering at the U of A, suggests it may be possible.

mix_items.title: Argentine researchers collect rodents for hantavirus tests
mix_items.summary: Argentine scientists on Tuesday began collecting rodents in the woods around Ushuaia to search for carriers of hantavirus in the area from which the virus-stricken MV Hondius set sail.

mix_items.title: Zoo reaches historic milestone for Puerto Rican crested toad conservation efforts with more than 12,000 tadpoles
mix_items.summary: Behind the scenes at Brookfield Zoo Chicago, a record-breaking conservation milestone is helping secure the future of one of the world's most imperiled amphibians. Months of meticulous care and coordination enabled Brookfield Zoo Chicago to successfully breed and raise 12,244 Puerto Rican crested toad tadpoles to be released in the wild, supporting species recovery efforts.

mix_items.title: Outbreak of rare Ebola's global risk is low but worries mount about its spread in Congo
mix_items.summary: The new outbreak of a rare Ebola type in Congo and Uganda poses low global risk, the World Health Organization said Wednesday, but responders on the ground described being underprotected and undertrained for a rapidly spreading virus with no approved medicine or vaccine.

mix_items.title: Q&A: What is in the new American Heart Association dietary recommendations?
mix_items.summary: Heart disease is the leading cause of death in the United States, and altering your diet is the most effective way to reduce the risk of heart disease, according to the American Heart Association. To that end, the American Heart Association regularly issues dietary recommendations for maintaining a healthy heart. Kristina Petersen, associate professor of nutritional sciences at Penn State, was part of the team of researchers who drafted the new recommendations.

mix_items.title: Sky bridges, citizen science protect endangered Malaysia monkeys
mix_items.summary: A graceful black monkey edges across a swaying red rope bridge strung over a busy residential road in Malaysia's Penang, watched by local conservationists who carefully record her movements.

mix_items.title: Scientists use light to create tiny molecules that could transform medicine
mix_items.summary: Researchers have developed a light-driven method for creating tiny, high-energy “housane” molecules that are valuable for drug development and materials science. These compact ring-shaped structures are difficult to produce because of the intense internal strain they contain. By using photocatalysis and carefully tuning the starting molecules, the team managed to guide the reaction into a clean and efficient pathway.

mix_items.title: Scientists found a giant magnetic “twist” hidden inside the Milky Way
mix_items.summary: Astronomers have uncovered a strange magnetic “flip” hidden inside the Milky Way. Using a new radio telescope, researchers mapped the galaxy’s magnetic field in unprecedented detail and discovered that a mysterious reversal in the Sagittarius Arm cuts diagonally across space. The finding could reshape how scientists understand the structure and future evolution of our galaxy.

mix_items.title: Britain’s 11,000-year-old “oldest northerner” was a 3-year-old girl, DNA reveals
mix_items.summary: Scientists have identified the oldest known human remains in Northern Britain as a young girl who lived around 11,000 years ago. Found in a Cumbrian cave and nicknamed the “Ossick Lass,” she was likely between 2.5 and 3.5 years old when she died. Nearby jewelry and evidence of multiple burials suggest the cave held deep spiritual importance for some of Britain’s earliest hunter-gatherers. The discovery is shedding new light on life — and death — just after the Ice Age.

mix_items.title: A new approach to fostering is taking shape in England
mix_items.summary: It takes a village to raise a child, or so the proverb goes. A new approach to fostering is embracing that idea The post A new approach to fostering is taking shape in England appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Scientists discover giant “last titan” dinosaur, Southeast Asia’s largest ever
mix_items.summary: A massive new dinosaur discovered in Thailand is rewriting Southeast Asia’s prehistoric history. The newly named Nagatitan chaiyaphumensis was a colossal long-necked sauropod that weighed around 27 tonnes and lived more than 100 million years ago. Scientists believe it may be the last giant sauropod ever to roam the region before rising seas transformed the landscape.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover vitamin B2 may help cancer cells survive
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising dark side to vitamin B2: it may help cancer cells stay alive. The vitamin supports a cellular shield that protects tumors from ferroptosis, a form of programmed cell death linked to cancer suppression. In lab tests, researchers used a vitamin B2-like compound called roseoflavin to break down that protection and trigger cancer cell death.

mix_items.title: The brain’s “feel good” chemical may be secretly fueling tinnitus
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered evidence that serotonin — the same brain chemical boosted by many antidepressants — may actually worsen tinnitus. Using advanced light-based brain stimulation in mice, researchers identified a serotonin-driven circuit linked directly to tinnitus-like behavior. The findings may explain why some people experience louder ringing in their ears while taking SSRIs.

mix_items.title: NASA’s Roman Space Telescope could reveal millions of invisible neutron stars
mix_items.summary: NASA’s Roman Space Telescope could expose a vast hidden population of neutron stars lurking unseen across the Milky Way. By detecting subtle shifts in starlight caused by gravity, the mission may identify and even weigh isolated neutron stars that are otherwise impossible to see. Scientists hope the discoveries will reveal how these extreme objects are born and why they are blasted through space at incredible speeds.

mix_items.title: Scientists successfully transfer longevity gene and extend lifespan
mix_items.summary: Scientists at the University of Rochester pulled off a remarkable experiment: they transferred a longevity-related gene from the famously long-lived naked mole rat into mice, and the mice ended up healthier and lived longer. The special gene boosts production of a substance called high molecular weight hyaluronic acid, which appears to protect against cancer, reduce inflammation, and support healthier aging. The modified mice showed stronger resistance to tumors, healthier guts, and lower levels of age-related inflammation.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am facut curaj. L-am lasat pe canapea si am plecat fara el.

satire_ro.title: Wiz Khalifa, urmărit general în România. Un poliţist şi-a spart deja televizorul încercând să-l prindă
